flashcards.front: What percentage of infant mortality is attributed to developmental and genetic birth defects in Western countries?
flashcards.back: About 50% of total mortality in infancy and childhood [1].
The branch of science dealing with the study of developmental anomalies during foetal life [2].

flashcards.front: What is the outcome of teratogen exposure during the first trimester of pregnancy?
flashcards.back: It is the intrauterine stage at which most teratogens induce birth defects [2].
The complete absence of an organ, such as unilateral or bilateral absence of the kidney [20].
The absence of development of an organ with the presence of a rudiment or anlage [20].
Incomplete development of an organ that does not reach normal adult size [20].
Incomplete formation of a lumen in a hollow viscus, such as oesophageal atresia [20].

flashcards.front: What is Developmental Dysplasia in the context of foetal life?
flashcards.back: Defective development of cells and tissues resulting in abnormal or primitive histogenetic structures, like renal dysplasia [26].
Defects resulting from failure of fusion, such as spina bifida [26].
The abnormal location of tissue at an ectopic site, such as pancreatic tissue in the stomach wall [26].

flashcards.front: What clinical condition resulted from the use of Thalidomide in the 1960s?
flashcards.back: Phocomelia, which is a high incidence of limb-reduction anomalies [27].

flashcards.front: What are the features of Foetal Alcohol Syndrome?
flashcards.back: Increased risk of miscarriages, stillbirths, growth retardation, and mental retardation [25].

flashcards.front: What organisms constitute the TORCH complex?
flashcards.back: Toxoplasma, Others, Rubella, Cytomegalovirus, and Herpes simplex [25].

flashcards.front: What is Hutchinson’s triad in congenital syphilis?
flashcards.back: Interstitial keratitis, sensorineural deafness, and deformed Hutchinson’s teeth [28].
The branch of science dealing with the study of human chromosomal abnormalities [21].
The inactivation of one of the two X chromosomes in females during embryogenesis [21].
An inactive X chromosome in female somatic cells that lies condensed in the nucleus [21].

flashcards.front: What minimum percentage of cells must be sex chromatin positive to indicate genetic female composition?
flashcards.back: A minimum of 30% [21].
The photographic representation of the stained preparation of chromosomes [21].

flashcards.front: Distinguish between p-arm and q-arm of a chromosome.
flashcards.back: The p-arm is the short arm (petit), and the q-arm is the long arm [16].
They have very short arms and the centromere is eccentrically located (e.g., numbers 13, 14, 15, 21, 22, and Y) [16].
A chromosomal number that is a multiple of the haploid number, such as triploid (69) or tetraploid (92) [29].
A chromosomal number that is not an exact multiple of the haploid number, such as 45 (monosomy) or 47 (trisomy) [8].

flashcards.front: What is the most common mechanism of aneuploidy?
flashcards.back: Nondisjunction, the failure of chromosomes to separate normally during cell division [8].
An individual having two or more types of cell lines derived from the same zygote, often due to mitotic nondisjunction [30].

flashcards.front: What is the chromosomal abnormality in 95% of Down’s syndrome cases?
flashcards.back: Trisomy 21 due to meiotic nondisjunction [9].

flashcards.front: What is the karyotype for Klinefelter’s syndrome?
flashcards.back: Typically 47, XXY [10].

flashcards.front: What is the karyotype for Turner’s syndrome?
flashcards.back: 45, X0, often due to loss of the X chromosome in paternal meiosis [10].

flashcards.front: What is a Balanced structural alteration of chromosomes?
flashcards.back: A rearrangement where there is no change in the total number of genes or genetic material [10].
The exchange of genetic material between two non-homologous chromosomes without involving the centromere [12].
The fusion of two acrocentric chromosomes at the centromere with the loss of their short arms [15].

flashcards.front: What chromosomal deletion causes Cri du chat syndrome?
flashcards.back: Deletion of the short arm of chromosome 5 (5p-) [15].

flashcards.front: What chromosomal deletion is associated with Retinoblastoma?
flashcards.back: Deletion of the long arm of chromosome 13 [15].

flashcards.front: What chromosomal deletion is associated with Wilms’ tumour?
flashcards.back: Deletion of the short arm of chromosome 11 [15].
A chromosome formed when the centromere divides transverse to the long axis, resulting in two identical arms [31].
A permanent change in the DNA of a cell [32].
The substitution of a single nucleotide base by a different base (e.g., sickle cell anaemia) [33].

flashcards.front: What is a Nonsense (Stop Codon) Mutation?
flashcards.back: A mutation that prematurely terminates or truncates the protein chain [33].
The insertion or deletion of one or two base pairs in the DNA sequence [33].
When both alleles of a gene pair are expressed in the heterozygous state [34].

flashcards.front: What is the chance of passing an autosomal dominant disease to offspring?
What is the chance of offspring having an autosomal recessive disease if both parents are carriers?
List three examples of Autosomal Dominant disorders.
flashcards.back: Familial polyposis coli, Adult polycystic kidney, and Marfan’s syndrome [36, 37].

flashcards.front: List three examples of X-linked Recessive inheritance.
flashcards.back: Haemophilia A, G6PD deficiency, and Colour blindness [37].
Disorders resulting from the combined effect of genetic composition and environmental influences [38].

flashcards.front: Why are storage diseases called 'lysosomal storage diseases'?
flashcards.back: Because lysosomes are the chief site of intracellular digestion, and metabolites accumulate there due to enzyme defects [4].

flashcards.front: What enzyme is deficient in von Gierke’s disease (Type I)?
flashcards.back: Glucose-6-phosphatase [22, 39].
Deficiency of acid maltase, leading to glycogen accumulation in heart and muscle (cardiomegaly) [22, 23].

flashcards.front: What enzyme is deficient in Gaucher’s disease?
flashcards.back: Acid b-glucosidase (glucocerebrosidase) [5, 40].
A distended macrophage with fibrillar cytoplasm resembling crumpled tissue paper [17].
Accumulation of sphingomyelin and cholesterol due to acid sphingomyelinase defect [5, 41].

flashcards.front: Where is the 'cherry-red spot' found in Niemann-Pick Type A?
flashcards.back: In the macula of the retina [41].
The time from birth to the first 4 weeks of life [5].
An embryonal tumour formed by the proliferation of undifferentiated or partially differentiated stem cells [11].
A focal accumulation of cells normally present in that tissue but arranged in an abnormal manner [13].

flashcards.front: What are the most common malignant tumours in children under 4 years old?
flashcards.back: Various types of blastomas (e.g., neuroblastoma, nephroblastoma, retinoblastoma) [7, 42].
